name: Save form submissions with Airtable and Gmail

parameters.formTitle: Save form submissions with Airtable and Gmail

parameters.content:
### New Lead Form Submission

Serves an n8n-hosted form (Email, Company, Score). Each submission becomes one execution.

Next: Route by Work Type

### Route by Work Type

Routes each item to one of 4 lanes: standard, priority, vip + fallback.

Ports: ①→ Process 'standard' → Airtable · ②→ Fast-Track 'priority' + Alert · ③→ Fast-Track 'vip' + Alert · ④→ Hold Unknown Types (DLQ)

### Process 'standard' → Airtable

Upserts into Airtable's 'Leads' table, matched on `leadId` — safe to re-run without creating duplicates.

Next: Merge Routed Work

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 Airtable PAT

### Fast-Track 'priority' + Alert

Sends an email via Gmail — subject is the first line of the message body, recipient comes from the item's `email` field.

Next: Merge Routed Work

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 Google OAuth2

### Fast-Track 'vip' + Alert

Sends an email via Gmail — subject is the first line of the message body, recipient comes from the item's `email` field.

Next: Merge Routed Work

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 Google OAuth2

### Hold Unknown Types (DLQ)

Creates a new record in Airtable's 'Leads' table.

Next: Merge Routed Work

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 Airtable PAT

### Merge Routed Work

Rejoins 4 branches into one stream (mode: append) so downstream logic runs once.

Next: Cap Batch Volume

### Cap Batch Volume

Caps the stream at 100 items to protect downstream quotas.

Next: Align Field Names

### Align Field Names

Normalizes field names so every source feeds the same downstream schema.

# Save form submissions with Airtable and Gmail

**Pattern:** Form Intake · **Level:** Foundation · **Trigger:** Form

## What it does
Save form submissions with Airtable and Gmail. It starts from a hosted form submission.

## Why it exists
Manual enrichment is slow and error-prone; this workflow makes the process repeatable, monitored and hands-off, with failures surfaced instead of silently lost.

## Main flow
New Lead Form Submission → Route by Work Type → Process 'standard' → Airtable → Merge Routed Work → Cap Batch Volume → Align Field Names

## Use it for
- Teams that want to form intake without manual effort
- Anyone automating enrichment in sales
- Replacing a fragile manual process with a reliable workflow

## You get
- Saves hours of manual work each week
- Has retries and error handling built in
- Removes copy-paste errors

## Before first run
Connect credentials: Airtable PAT; Google OAuth2.
Follow the color key: 🟦 entry · 🟪 AI · 🟫 actions · ⬜ utilities · 🟩 outputs · 🟥 error rail.
Each node has its own doc card underneath — read those before editing.

## Try it now
Sample data is pinned on the trigger — hit **Execute Workflow** to watch it flow through every node before connecting any real credentials. Unpin the trigger (right-click → Unpin) when you're ready to go live.

## Design decisions

Unrecognized work types are parked in a dead-letter lane instead of erroring the whole run or silently dropping the item — new event kinds will show up eventually, and this is where you'd notice one without losing it. One kind is deliberately routed through a sub-workflow to show how heavier per-type logic can be extracted without touching this router at all.

## Practice exercises

New to this pattern? Try these in order:

1. Send a work-type value that matches no switch rule and confirm it lands in the dead-letter branch.
2. Add a new work type and its own handling branch.
3. Replace the direct-write branch with a sub-workflow call, matching the pattern already used for the first kind.

## 🛟 When this fails

This starter workflow keeps things simple: no automatic retry or error branch yet. If a node fails, the execution just stops — open n8n's **Executions** tab to see which node and why. Ready to harden it? Turn on **Retry On Fail** (3 tries, 2s backoff) on the Settings tab of each external node, and add an error-output branch to a Slack/email alert — every Production-tier template in this library shows that exact pattern.

## 🛠 Customize me

Quick wins: point the data nodes at your own accounts (see 🔑 lines on each card), adjust the trigger cadence, and tune thresholds/labels to your enrichment process. Every node card explains its role — safe to extend at any marked branch.
